slides.title: Exploring Large Numbers
slides.text: Let's learn about whole numbers up to ten thousand. These numbers have four digits and help us count large collections of objects in daily life. In math, we express this as: Numbers up to 10,000 = big values. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Can you think of a real-world collection that has thousands of items?

examples.title: Example 1
examples.content: Review standard details: Numbers up to 10,000 = big values.

slides.title: Visualizing Thousands
slides.text: We can visualize numbers using base-ten blocks. A large block represents one thousand, which is made of ten flat hundreds blocks joined together. In math, we express this as: 1000 = 10 hundreds blocks. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. How many tens blocks do we need to make one thousand blocks?

examples.title: Example 2
examples.content: Review standard details: 1000 = 10 hundreds blocks.

slides.title: The Four-Digit Place Value Chart
slides.text: We organize four-digit numbers in a place value chart. The positions from right to left are Ones, Tens, Hundreds, and Thousands. In math, we express this as: Place values = Thousands, Hundreds, Tens, Ones. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Which digit in the number 4,567 is in the thousands place?

examples.title: Example 3
examples.content: Review standard details: Place values = Thousands, Hundreds, Tens, Ones.

slides.title: Understanding Place Value
slides.text: Place value is the position of a digit in a number. It tells us if the digit represents ones, tens, hundreds, or thousands. In math, we express this as: Place value of 5 in 5,432 = Thousands. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. What is the place value of the digit 3 in the number 8,312?

examples.title: Example 4
examples.content: Review standard details: Place value of 5 in 5,432 = Thousands.

slides.title: Calculating the Value of a Digit
slides.text: The value of a digit is found by multiplying the digit by its place value. For example, a 6 in the hundreds place has a value of 600. In math, we express this as: Value of 6 in 7,621 = 6 x 100 = 600. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. What is the value of the digit 4 in the number 4,921?

examples.title: Example 5
examples.content: Review standard details: Value of 6 in 7,621 = 6 x 100 = 600.

slides.title: Reading Numbers in Words
slides.text: When reading a number, we read the thousands place, say the word thousand, and then read the remaining three digits as a standard number. In math, we express this as: 4,520 = four thousand, five hundred twenty. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. How do you read the number 7,809 in words aloud?

examples.title: Example 6
examples.content: Review standard details: 4,520 = four thousand, five hundred twenty.

slides.title: Writing Numbers in Numerals
slides.text: When writing a number, we write the digits in their correct place value positions. We use a comma to separate the thousands from the hundreds. In math, we express this as: Three thousand, forty = 3,040. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. How do you write six thousand, five hundred twelve in numerals?

examples.title: Example 7
examples.content: Review standard details: Three thousand, forty = 3,040.

slides.title: The Expanded Form
slides.text: Expanded form shows a number written as the sum of the values of its digits. This helps us see the breakdown of thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. In math, we express this as: 5,234 = 5,000 + 200 + 30 + 4. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Can you write the number 8,740 in expanded form?

examples.title: Example 8
examples.content: Review standard details: 5,234 = 5,000 + 200 + 30 + 4.

slides.title: Pineapple Harvesting counts
slides.text: Farmers in Polomolok count their harvested sweet pineapples. A small farm plot can produce four thousand, five hundred pineapples in one week. In math, we express this as: Harvest = 4,500 pineapples in Polomolok. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. If a farmer harvests 4,500 pineapples, how many thousands are there?

examples.title: Example 9
examples.content: Review standard details: Harvest = 4,500 pineapples in Polomolok.

slides.title: Lake Sebu Tourist Tracking
slides.text: The tourism office in Lake Sebu tracks the number of visitors. Last month, eight thousand, one hundred twenty-five tourists visited the scenic waterfalls. In math, we express this as: Visitors = 8,125 tourists at waterfalls. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. How do you write the tourist number 8,125 in words?

examples.title: Example 10
examples.content: Review standard details: Visitors = 8,125 tourists at waterfalls.

slides.title: Barangay Population Records
slides.text: Barangay records in South Cotabato help us track resident counts. A local barangay has nine thousand, four hundred thirty residents living in the community. In math, we express this as: Population = 9,430 residents in South Cotabato. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. What is the value of the digit 9 in the population 9,430?

examples.title: Example 11
examples.content: Review standard details: Population = 9,430 residents in South Cotabato.

slides.title: What is Rounding?
slides.text: Rounding is finding a simpler number close to the actual value. It makes numbers easier to use and estimate in daily math problems. In math, we express this as: Rounding = finding the nearest benchmark number. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Why do we round numbers when estimating total costs?

examples.title: Example 12
examples.content: Review standard details: Rounding = finding the nearest benchmark number.

slides.title: Rounding to the Nearest Ten
slides.text: To round to the nearest ten, we look at the ones digit. If it is 5 or more, we round up; if it is less than 5, we round down. In math, we express this as: 4,527 rounded to tens = 4,530. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. What is 3,412 rounded to the nearest ten?

examples.title: Example 13
examples.content: Review standard details: 4,527 rounded to tens = 4,530.

slides.title: Rounding to the Nearest Hundred
slides.text: To round to the nearest hundred, we check the tens digit. Since 8 is 5 or more, we round the hundreds digit up to the next hundred. In math, we express this as: 6,781 rounded to hundreds = 6,800. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. What is 5,230 rounded to the nearest hundred?

examples.title: Example 14
examples.content: Review standard details: 6,781 rounded to hundreds = 6,800.

slides.title: Rounding to the Nearest Thousand
slides.text: To round to the nearest thousand, we check the hundreds digit. Since 2 is less than 5, we round down, keeping the thousands digit unchanged. In math, we express this as: 8,245 rounded to thousands = 8,000. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. What is 7,850 rounded to the nearest thousand?

examples.title: Example 15
examples.content: Review standard details: 8,245 rounded to thousands = 8,000.

slides.title: Comparing Four-Digit Numbers
slides.text: To compare two numbers, we look at the digits from left to right. We start with the thousands, then hundreds, tens, and ones. In math, we express this as: 8,450 vs 8,290: 8,450 is larger. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Which number is larger: 5,670 or 5,690?

examples.title: Example 16
examples.content: Review standard details: 8,450 vs 8,290: 8,450 is larger.

slides.title: Using Comparison Symbols
slides.text: We use relation symbols to show comparison: greater than (>), less than (<), and equal to (=). The open side of the symbol points to the larger number. In math, we express this as: 4,560 > 4,520. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. If the checking digit is five or more, we round up; if it is less than five, we round down. Which symbol fits in the blank: 8,901 ___ 8,910?

examples.title: Example 17
examples.content: Review standard details: 4,560 > 4,520.

slides.title: Ordering Numbers: Smallest to Largest
slides.text: Ordering in ascending order means listing numbers from the lowest value to the highest. We compare each number to find its correct position. In math, we express this as: 3,120 < 3,450 < 3,890. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Arrange these numbers in ascending order: 4,500, 4,200, 4,800.

examples.title: Example 18
examples.content: Review standard details: 3,120 < 3,450 < 3,890.

slides.title: Ordering Numbers: Largest to Smallest
slides.text: Ordering in descending order means listing numbers from the highest value to the lowest. We find the largest number first and write them down. In math, we express this as: 9,800 > 9,500 > 9,100. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Arrange these numbers in descending order: 6,100, 6,700, 6,400.

examples.title: Example 19
examples.content: Review standard details: 9,800 > 9,500 > 9,100.

slides.title: Finding Number Patterns
slides.text: We can count in patterns of tens, hundreds, or thousands. Recognizing patterns helps us count large quantities of items very quickly. In math, we express this as: Pattern: 1,000, 2,000, 3,000, 4,000. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. What is the next number in the pattern: 3,500, 4,500, 5,500?

examples.title: Example 20
examples.content: Review standard details: Pattern: 1,000, 2,000, 3,000, 4,000.

slides.title: Zero as a Placeholder
slides.text: Zero is a vital placeholder in our number system. It shows that a place value has no value, keeping other digits in their correct positions. In math, we express this as: 4,050 vs 4,500. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Why would a number change if we removed the zero placeholder?

examples.title: Example 21
examples.content: Review standard details: 4,050 vs 4,500.

slides.title: Counting Philippine Pesos
slides.text: We use large numbers when counting money. If you save five ₱1,000 bills, four ₱100 bills, and one ₱50 bill, you have ₱5,450. In math, we express this as: Money = ₱5,450 total savings. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. How do you write five thousand, four hundred fifty Pesos in numerals?

examples.title: Example 22
examples.content: Review standard details: Money = ₱5,450 total savings.

slides.title: Common Error: Writing Placeholders
slides.text: A common error is forgetting zeros when writing numbers. For example, writing five thousand, three as 53 instead of the correct 5,003. In math, we express this as: Five thousand, three = 5,003. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Why is it incorrect to write five thousand, three as 53?

examples.title: Example 23
examples.content: Review standard details: Five thousand, three = 5,003.

slides.title: Rounding and Comparing Problems
slides.text: Let's solve a problem. A school has 4,520 students. Round this number to the nearest thousand. Since the hundreds digit is 5, we round up to 5,000. In math, we express this as: Round 4,520 to nearest thousand = 5,000. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Is 8,500 rounded to the nearest thousand 8,000 or 9,000?

examples.title: Example 24
examples.content: Review standard details: Round 4,520 to nearest thousand = 5,000.

slides.title: Summary of Week 4 Lessons
slides.text: Let's review: we learned to represent, read, and write numbers up to ten thousand, identify place values, round to tens/hundreds/thousands, and compare/order numbers. In math, we express this as: Numbers to 10,000 = read, write, compare, round. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. How do large numbers help us record statistics in South Cotabato?

examples.title: Example 25
examples.content: Review standard details: Numbers to 10,000 = read, write, compare, round.

performance.title: Tuna Harvest Record Block Drawing

Draw 4 large cubes for thousands, 3 flat squares for hundreds, 2 vertical rectangles for tens, and 5 small dots for ones. Color them neatly, write the number in standard form and in words, take a photo, and upload it.

slides.title: Welcome to Process Skills!
slides.text: Hello, Scientist! Welcome to Science Week 4! This week we are mastering science process skills. Process skills are tools scientists use to explore nature. We will focus on three core skills today. These skills are observing, predicting, and measuring. We use units like millimeters, centimeters, and meters. Let's practice these skills in our simple activities. Are you ready to think like a professional investigator? For instance, consider the example of Goal: Describe and apply observing, predicting, and measuring process skills. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Goal
examples.content: Describe and apply observing, predicting, and measuring process skills.

slides.title: The Skill of Observing
slides.text: Observing is using your senses to gather facts. We observe colors, shapes, textures, and sounds. An observation must be factual and objective always. E.g., 'The metal coin sinks in the water cup.' Avoid personal opinions like 'The coin is pretty.' Use hand lenses to observe tiny details on leaves. Use touch to observe the stickiness of wet clay. Always record what you see, feel, hear, or smell! For instance, consider the example of Observation: Observing that clay holds shape under thumb pressure. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Observation
examples.content: Observing that clay holds shape under thumb pressure.

slides.title: The Skill of Predicting
slides.text: Predicting is making logical guesses about future events. It is not a wild guess; it requires evidence. We use clues and past experiences to predict outcomes. E.g., 'Dark grey clouds mean it will rain soon.' If you place water in freezer pots overnight, you predict it will turn to solid ice cubes. If you drop a heavy stone in water buckets, you predict it will sink to the bottom quickly! For instance, consider the example of Prediction: Predicting that wet clothes dry faster under hot sun. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Prediction
examples.content: Predicting that wet clothes dry faster under hot sun.

slides.title: The Skill of Measuring
slides.text: Measuring is using tools to collect numerical data. It helps us know the exact size or weight. We use rulers to measure length accurately. We measure in millimeters (mm) for tiny objects. We measure in centimeters (cm) for classroom items. We measure in meters (m) for larger room dimensions. Always align the object edge with the zero mark. Write numbers and units together in your logs! For instance, consider the example of Measurement: Measuring a seedling height as exactly 45 millimeters. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Measurement
examples.content: Measuring a seedling height as exactly 45 millimeters.

slides.title: Millimeters, Centimeters, Meters
slides.text: Centimeters and millimeters are marked on rulers. There are ten millimeters in one single centimeter. We use millimeters for small insects or seeds. We use centimeters for books, pencils, and leaves. We use meters for classroom floors or chalkboards. One meter is equal to exactly 100 centimeters. Choosing the right unit makes your data precise. Measure your pinky fingernail width in millimeters now! For instance, consider the example of Units: 1 cm = 10 mm, 1 m = 100 cm. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

slides.title: Observing Solid Properties
slides.text: We practice observing physical properties of solids. Solid materials hold their own shapes and volumes. Observe a metal nail, a cotton ball, and stones. We classify them by hardness, shininess, and texture. The rock is hard; the cotton is soft to touch. The metal is shiny; the charcoal is dull grey. These observations describe the physical state of solids. Record three solid properties in your notebook page! For instance, consider the example of Properties: Shiny metal nail, hard river stone, soft cotton fibers. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Properties
examples.content: Shiny metal nail, hard river stone, soft cotton fibers.

slides.title: Predicting Weather Changes
slides.text: Weather prediction is a daily science process skill. We look at the sky to gather weather clues. Dark grey clouds block sunlight, cooling the air. Strong winds blow dust and sway tree branches. Based on these clues, we predict a rain storm. Our past experience tells us grey clouds bring rain. Predicting helps communities prepare for weather events. Write down your weather prediction for tomorrow afternoon! For instance, consider the example of Weather: Predicting rain when humidity rises and clouds darken. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Weather
examples.content: Predicting rain when humidity rises and clouds darken.

slides.title: Measuring Plant Growth
slides.text: Measuring seedling growth requires precision and care. Place a plastic ruler next to sprout stems. Align the zero mark with the soil level. Read the height to the nearest millimeter line. Write down the height in your growth chart daily. Comparing daily height reveals the growth rate. A watered seedling grew 3 millimeters in one day. Measuring helps us test if watering works well! For instance, consider the example of Plant Measure: Seedling height measured at 12 mm on Day 3. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Plant Measure
examples.content: Seedling height measured at 12 mm on Day 3.

slides.title: Observing water evaporation
slides.text: Let's observe water turning into invisible gas vapor. Drip a water drop on a warm chalkboard. Watch closely as the wet spot shrinks soon. The spot disappears completely in a few minutes. This observation shows evaporation in action today. Heat from room air turned liquid into gas vapor. Observe how long a wet tissue takes to dry. Write down the drying time in minutes in logs! For instance, consider the example of Evaporation: Liquid water spot vanishes from warm blackboard surfaces. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Evaporation
examples.content: Liquid water spot vanishes from warm blackboard surfaces.

slides.title: Predicting Shadow Directions
slides.text: Shadow directions change in regular daily patterns. The Sun rises in the east in the morning. It casts shadows pointing towards the west side. In the afternoon, the Sun sets in the west. It casts shadows pointing towards the east side. Predict where your shadow will fall at noon today. At noon, the Sun is directly above your head. So the shadow will be short and under feet! For instance, consider the example of Shadows: Morning shadows point west, afternoon shadows point east. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Shadows
examples.content: Morning shadows point west, afternoon shadows point east.

slides.title: Measuring Liquid Volume
slides.text: We measure liquid volume in milliliters in labs. We use graduated cylinders or cups with markings. Place the cup on flat desk surfaces to read. Look at the liquid surface at eye level closely. The liquid curves slightly, forming a meniscus curve. Read the number at the bottom of the curve. Accurate volume measures are key for chemical tests. Measure 50 milliliters of water for your plants! For instance, consider the example of Volume: Measuring water volume at 50 mL mark in cylinders. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Volume
examples.content: Measuring water volume at 50 mL mark in cylinders.

slides.title: Observing Force Effects
slides.text: We observe how pushes and pulls change movement. Push a toy car slowly across smooth desks. It rolls forward and stops due to table friction. Now push it harder with a strong finger force. Observe that it rolls faster and goes further. The amount of force changes speed and distance. Measuring distance in centimeters shows force effects. Record push distance for three tests in charts! For instance, consider the example of Force Test: Soft push = 10 cm, hard push = 30 cm distance. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Force Test
examples.content: Soft push = 10 cm, hard push = 30 cm distance.

slides.title: Predicting Material Changes
slides.text: We predict how heat changes different solid materials. Place solid wax, butter, and stones in sun. We predict the wax and butter will melt soon. We predict the stone will not melt in sun. Past tests show organic solids melt at low heat. Stones require extreme furnace heat to change state. This prediction uses physical facts of melting points. Check your predictions after one hour of exposure! For instance, consider the example of Melting: Predicting butter melts while rocks remain solid in sun. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Melting
examples.content: Predicting butter melts while rocks remain solid in sun.

slides.title: Measuring Room Dimensions
slides.text: We use tape measures to measure larger room lengths. Rulers are too small for measuring classroom walls. We select meters (m) as the correct unit helper. Two students hold the ends of tape measures flat. Read the measurement line next to the corner wall. E.g., the classroom length is exactly 8 meters. Recording dimensions helps us plan desk layouts. Measure the length of your chalkboard in meters today! For instance, consider the example of Meters: Chalkboard length measured at 3 meters and 20 cm. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Meters
examples.content: Chalkboard length measured at 3 meters and 20 cm.

slides.title: Observing Animal Behaviors
slides.text: Let's observe behavior patterns of garden ants. Ants walk in straight lines along wall cracks. They carry food bits back to their nest holes. Observe how they touch antennae when they meet. Antennae touches share information about food locations. This is a factual observation using sight organs. Write down ant behaviors without adding opinion words. Keep your animal observation log completely objective! For instance, consider the example of Ants: Ants carry bread crumbs three times their body size. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Ants
examples.content: Ants carry bread crumbs three times their body size.

slides.title: Predicting Plant Survival
slides.text: We predict plant survival under different conditions. Place one plant in sun, one in dark closets. Water both plants with equal water amounts daily. We predict the plant in the closet will weaken. It will turn yellow and die due to lack of sun. The plant in the sun will grow healthy leaves. This prediction uses plant biology facts we learned. Verify the leaf color in both pots after weeks! For instance, consider the example of Survival: Closet plant turns yellow due to lack of light energy. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Survival
examples.content: Closet plant turns yellow due to lack of light energy.

slides.title: Measuring temperature changes
slides.text: We measure heat levels using liquid thermometers. Thermometers contain liquid that rises when heated. We measure temperature in degrees Celsius (°C). Place the glass bulb in warm water cups closely. Watch the red liquid rise along the scale lines. Read the line number when the liquid stops rising. E.g., the warm water is at 45 degrees Celsius. Record temperature data carefully in your log book! For instance, consider the example of Temperature: Measuring room temperature at 25 degrees Celsius. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Temperature
examples.content: Measuring room temperature at 25 degrees Celsius.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Observing
slides.text: Let's observe physical properties of kitchen liquids. We examine cooking oil, water, and sweet syrup. Oil is yellow; water is clear and colorless. Syrup is dark brown and flows very slowly. We observe the thickness or viscosity of liquids. Slow flowing shows high viscosity in daily science. Fast flowing shows low viscosity (like clean water). Record these liquid properties in your journal page! For instance, consider the example of Liquids: Syrup flows slower than water because it is thick. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Liquids
examples.content: Syrup flows slower than water because it is thick.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Predicting
slides.text: Let's practice predicting results of mixing colors. We mix blue dye and yellow dye in cups. What color do you predict the mixture will turn? Based on coloring experiences, we predict green! Stir the liquid and observe the color change. The liquid turns green, confirming our prediction. Predictions help us test if color rules work. Predict what mixing red and yellow creates today! For instance, consider the example of Color Test: Blue + Yellow = Green is a verified prediction. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Color Test
examples.content: Blue + Yellow = Green is a verified prediction.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Measuring
slides.text: Let's practice measuring water drops on coins. Use a plastic eye dropper to drip water on coins. Count how many drops a one-peso coin holds. Drip slowly and observe water dome forming. The coin holds 25 drops before spilling over table. Measuring drops count shows water surface tension. Repeat the test three times to get average data. Record each drop count in your science log! For instance, consider the example of Drops: Test 1 = 24 drops, Test 2 = 25 drops, Test 3 = 26 drops. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Drops
examples.content: Test 1 = 24 drops, Test 2 = 25 drops, Test 3 = 26 drops.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Process Skills
slides.text: Let's combine all process skills in one lab. Observe a solid candle on your laboratory desk. Predict what happens when wicks are lit with fire. We predict solid wax will melt and turn liquid. Light the wick and measure melting time with clocks. The wax turns to liquid in exactly two minutes. Record the observation, prediction, and measured time. Combining skills is how scientists perform investigations! For instance, consider the example of Combined Skills: Observed solid wax, predicted melting, measured time. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Combined Skills
examples.content: Observed solid wax, predicted melting, measured time.

slides.title: Common Error: Subjective Observations
slides.text: Avoid using subjective words in scientific logs. Do not write: 'The ant is cute and works hard.' Cute and hard-working are opinions, not measurements. Instead, write: 'The ant carried 5-gram crumbs.' This statement contains measurable and factual details. Keep descriptions clear of personal feelings always. It ensures other scientists can repeat your work. Check your data sheets to remove subjective terms today! For instance, consider the example of Incorrect: Writing 'This flower smells the best' in logs. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Incorrect
examples.content: Writing 'This flower smells the best' in logs.

slides.title: Common Error: Unreasonable Predictions
slides.text: Avoid making predictions that do not match facts. Do not predict: 'A dry seed in sand grows tomorrow.' This guess violates plant biology rules of growth. Seeds need water and time to germinate and sprout. A reasonable prediction is: 'Seeds sprout in five days.' Ensure your predictions are backed by evidence and logic. Unreasonable guesses make your reports look unscientific. Review your predictions using science rules first! For instance, consider the example of Incorrect: Predicting that dropping rocks makes them float on water. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Incorrect
examples.content: Predicting that dropping rocks makes them float on water.

slides.title: Extra Practice: Science Review A
slides.text: Let's review the important science concepts of Week 4. We have studied observations, measurements, and predictions. A real scientist is always curious and checks their facts. Think about the experiments we discussed in the slides. Can you explain them in your own words to others? Use your scientific vocabulary to describe your ideas clearly. Answer the quiz questions and challenges to test your memory. Keep up the great work and enjoy exploring the world! For instance, consider the example of Review Card 1: Practice explaining these science ideas to your family. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Review Card 1
examples.content: Practice explaining these science ideas to your family.

slides.title: Summary of Week 4
slides.text: Congratulations! You completed all the slides for Week 4. Let's review the main science tools we mastered. We learned to apply observing, predicting, and measuring. We used millimeters, centimeters, and meters on rulers. We measured temperatures in Celsius and liquid volumes. We predicted weather changes and plant survival rates. We avoided subjective observations and unreasonable guesses. You are now ready to take the quiz and challenges! For instance, consider the example of Key Takeaway: Process skills are the foundation of scientific thinking. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Key Takeaway
examples.content: Process skills are the foundation of scientific thinking.

standard.q: Which science process skill uses your senses to collect facts?
Which statement is a factual scientific observation?
options: The plant stem is 15 centimeters tall.
Plants are the best living things.
I like green leaves more than red ones.

standard.q: What is a logical prediction when dark grey clouds cover the sky?
Which tool is used to measure air temperature in degrees Celsius?
What unit is best to measure the thickness of a small seed?
How many millimeters are equal to exactly one centimeter?
What is a logical prediction for water placed in a freezer overnight?
options: It will turn into solid ice.

standard.q: Which statement is a subjective opinion, NOT an observation?
options: The stone is grey and rough.

standard.q: What unit is best to measure the length of a classroom chalkboard?
True or False: A scientific prediction is a guess based on evidence and clues.
Which skill are you using when you count the drops of water on a coin?
Which of these is the correct symbol for degrees Celsius?
What happens to liquid water on a warm blackboard surface?
What shadow direction is cast when the Sun is rising in the East?
True or False: One meter is equal to exactly 10 centimeters.
Which property of thick syrup makes it flow slower than water?
What is the dependent variable in a plant growth experiment?
What is the independent variable in a plant growth experiment?
options: The factor you change (e.g. fertilizer amount)

standard.q: True or False: Observing that a rock feels rough is a qualitative observation.
Which VCV word represents the tool used to measure temperature?
What will happen to an iron nail left outside in wet air for weeks?
options: It will rust and turn orange-brown.

standard.q: Which skill is used to group objects by common properties?
True or False: Predicting that heavy rocks will float is reasonable based on daily evidence.
Which unit is used to measure liquid volume in lab cylinders?
What is the main purpose of using process skills in science?
options: To study nature systematically using evidence

challenge.q: Challenge: A student measures a leaf at 54 mm. How many centimeters and millimeters is this?
Challenge: If you predict that a seed will sprout faster in warm soil than cold soil, what is your evidence?
options: Heat energy increases biological activity speeds.

challenge.q: Challenge: Which variable should be controlled (kept the same) when testing if different soils affect plant growth?
options: The amount of water and sunlight given to all pots

challenge.q: Challenge: Why is the prediction 'the sun will rise in the west tomorrow' unreasonable?
options: It violates the established fact of Earth's rotation direction.
The sun only rises on weekdays.
It is too hot in the west.

challenge.q: Challenge: Which process skill combination is used when you measure a rusted area on an iron plate?
options: Observing the rust, and measuring its width in millimeters.
Predicting rain, and using a thermometer.
Tinkering with clay, and blowing balloons.

performance.title: Science Process Skills Home Lab
performance.desc: Select a solid object at home (like a key or a stone). 1. Observe and write down three qualitative properties (color, texture, shape). 2. Measure its length in millimeters and centimeters using a ruler. 3. Predict whether it will float or sink in water, then perform the test to verify. Record all steps and results in your science log.
labels: Conducted qualitative observations using senses
Measured object length accurately in cm and mm
Made and tested a logical float/sink prediction based on facts

pages:
Exercise 1: Science Process Skills

Write the correct process skill (Observing, Predicting, Measuring) for each action.

1. Recording that a chemical solution turned from blue to yellow:
Observing
2. Using a thermometer to check air temperature in degrees Celsius:
Measuring
3. Writing 'The ice will melt soon in the sun' before placing it outside:
Predicting

Exercise 2: Unit Matching

Write the correct unit abbreviation (mm, cm, m, °C, mL) for each measurement.

1. The width of your thumb nail (small size):
mm
2. The volume of water in a lab cylinder:
mL
3. The height of the classroom wall:
m

Exercise 3: Factual Observations

Identify whether each sentence is a Fact or an Opinion.

1. The green leaf has a length of 8 centimeters.
Fact
2. Spiders are the scariest creatures in the garden.
Opinion
3. The metal gate turned orange-brown and rough due to rust.
Fact

english.title: English
english.subtitle: Week 4: Describing Physical Properties & Sentence Review

slides.title: Welcome to the Review Expedition!
slides.text: Hello, Explorer! Welcome to English Week 4! This week, we are going on a review expedition. We will review our three spelling patterns: CVCC, CCVC, and VCV. We will learn about adjectives and how they describe things. We will review declarative, interrogative, and imperative sentences. We will make logical predictions based on clues in nature. Let's keep our notebooks ready to write down answers. Are you ready to show what you have learned? For instance, consider the example of Weekly Goal: Master spelling patterns review, apply adjectives to physical properties, review sentence types, and make predictions. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Weekly Goal
examples.content: Master spelling patterns review, apply adjectives to physical properties, review sentence types, and make predictions.

slides.title: Word Pattern Review
slides.text: We have learned three important word patterns this term. CVCC words end in two consonant letters (like hand). CCVC words start with two consonant letters (like frog). VCV words have a consonant between two vowels (like baby). Being able to spell them helps us read quickly. It also helps us write science observations accurately. Let's practice spelling these patterns on our paper. They are the building blocks of our language safari! For instance, consider the example of Review Patterns: CVCC (consonant blends), CCVC (initial blends), VCV (long vowels). To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Review Patterns
examples.content: CVCC (consonant blends), CCVC (initial blends), VCV (long vowels).

slides.title: CVCC Practice: The Wet Pond
slides.text: Let's walk near the wet pond in the park. We see a green lamp lighting up the path. Can you spot the CVCC words in our sentences? Yes, the words are 'pond' and 'lamp'! Both words end with a blend of two consonants. We spell them correctly as p-o-n-d and l-a-m-p. Listen to the ending sound of both words carefully. They show how CVCC blends are used in nature! For instance, consider the example of Spelling: p - o - n - d and l - a - m - p To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: p - o - n - d  and  l - a - m - p

slides.title: CCVC Practice: The Crawling Crab
slides.text: We spot a small crab crawling on the beach. It does not stop when it sees us watching. Can you spot the CCVC words in our sentences? Yes, the words are 'crab' and 'stop'! Both words start with a blend of two consonants. We spell them correctly as c-r-a-b and s-t-o-p. Listen to the beginning sound of both words carefully. They show how CCVC blends are used in nature! For instance, consider the example of Spelling: c - r - a - b and s - t - o - p To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: c - r - a - b  and  s - t - o - p

slides.title: VCV Practice: The Busy Spider
slides.text: We watch a busy spider spin a web on a tulip. It sits on the paper leaf in the warm sun. Can you spot the VCV words in our sentences? Yes, the words are 'spider', 'tulip', and 'paper'! They have a single consonant between two vowels. We spell them correctly as s-p-i-d-e-r and t-u-l-i-p. Listen to the long vowel sounds in these words. They show how VCV patterns are used in nature! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Spelling
examples.content: s-p-i-d-e-r and t-u-l-i-p

slides.title: Grammar: Describing Words (Adjectives)
slides.text: Adjectives are words that describe nouns in a sentence. They tell us about size, color, shape, or texture. They also tell us how things feel, taste, or look. Using adjectives makes our descriptions very clear and interesting. E.g., 'The green leaf' or 'The small frog'. The words 'green' and 'small' are adjectives. They help us paint a picture in the reader's mind. Let's find adjectives in the sentences we read today! For instance, consider the example of Key Concept: Adjective = Describing word To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Key Concept
examples.content: Adjective = Describing word

slides.title: Describing Physical Properties
slides.text: In science, we use adjectives to describe solid materials. We observe physical properties using our five sense organs. 'The hard rock does not bend or break.' 'The shiny metal nail reflects the sun light.' 'The sticky clay can be molded into shapes.' The adjectives are hard, shiny, and sticky. They describe the physical properties of each material. Write these describing words in your science log today! For instance, consider the example of Properties: hard rock, sticky clay, shiny metal, soft cotton To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Properties
examples.content: hard rock, sticky clay, shiny metal, soft cotton

slides.title: Adjectives of Size and Number
slides.text: Adjectives can also tell us about size and number. They help us count and measure objects we observe. 'Matteo found ten small seeds in the garden.' 'We saw three large carabaos near the river.' The adjectives are ten, small, three, and large. They tell us the quantity and size of the nouns. Using these adjectives helps us record accurate science data. How many describing words can you find in your room? To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Size
examples.content: small

examples.title: Number
examples.content: ten

slides.title: Sentence Review: Declarative
slides.text: Let's review the three types of sentences we learned. A declarative sentence tells a fact or makes a statement. E.g., 'The sun warms the loam soil in spring.' It always starts with a capital letter in writing. It always ends with a period punctuation mark. It has a subject (who) and a predicate (action). Most sentences in our science logs are declarative. Let's practice writing a declarative sentence about nature! For instance, consider the example of Example: Plants need water to grow. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Example
examples.content: Plants need water to grow.

slides.title: Sentence Review: Interrogative
slides.text: An interrogative sentence asks a question to gather facts. E.g., 'Does the plant bend towards the sunny window?' It always starts with a capital letter in writing. It always ends with a question mark (?) in writing. Scientists use interrogative sentences to start experiments. They ask questions about why things change in nature. Always check the question mark at the end of questions. It is the key punctuation for interrogative sentences! For instance, consider the example of Example: Why is the soil wet? To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Example
examples.content: Why is the soil wet?

slides.title: Sentence Review: Imperative
slides.text: An imperative sentence gives a command or makes a request. E.g., 'Please record the temperature in your log.' It always starts with a capital letter in writing. It usually ends with a period punctuation mark. If it is a polite request, it contains 'please'. We use imperatives to write steps for an experiment. They tell the reader what action they must perform. Let's write a polite request to clean our tools! For instance, consider the example of Example: Observe the frog carefully. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Example
examples.content: Observe the frog carefully.

slides.title: Story Time: The Gathering Clouds
slides.text: Let's read a short story to make a prediction: 'Matteo walks in the green garden after lunch. The blue sky suddenly turns dark grey with clouds. Strong winds begin to blow the leaves of trees. Large drops of water fall from the heavy clouds.' This story has clues about what will happen next. Let's learn how to make a logical prediction. It is a very important skill for language and science! For instance, consider the example of Reading Task: Gather clues to make a prediction about what happens next! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Reading Task
examples.content: Gather clues to make a prediction about what happens next!

slides.title: Predicting Endings: What is It?
slides.text: Predicting means making a guess about what happens next. It is not a wild guess; it must be logical. We use clues from the story to guide us. We also use our own experiences in daily life. Clues + Experience = A good and logical prediction. It helps us anticipate outcomes and explain natural cycles. Let's look at the clues in Matteo's garden story. What will happen next in the garden? For instance, consider the example of Inquiry: Clues + Experience = Logical Prediction To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Inquiry
examples.content: Clues + Experience = Logical Prediction

slides.title: Clues in the Sky
slides.text: What are the clues in Matteo's garden story? The sky is dark grey with heavy rain clouds. Strong winds are blowing the leaves of trees. Drops of water are beginning to fall from above. Based on these clues, we predict it will rain. This prediction is logical and matches the evidence. Predicting a hot sunny day would not make sense. Always look for evidence before you make a prediction! For instance, consider the example of Prediction: It will rain soon. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Prediction
examples.content: It will rain soon.

slides.title: Logic and Evidence
slides.text: Good predictions must always match the physical facts. In science, we call this predicting based on evidence. If you drop a heavy metal ball into water, will it float on top or sink to the bottom? Based on evidence, you predict it will sink. If you leave an iron nail outside in the rain, you predict it will rust and turn orange-brown. Both predictions are logical because they use physical facts! For instance, consider the example of Evidence: Dark clouds usually bring rain, not sunshine. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Evidence
examples.content: Dark clouds usually bring rain, not sunshine.

slides.title: Vocabulary: Synonyms
slides.text: Synonyms are words that have the same meaning. They help us express ideas in different ways. Using synonyms makes our writing rich and interesting. E.g., the words 'large' and 'big' are synonyms. The words 'quiet' and 'silent' are also synonyms. Both words describe the same state or size in text. Let's find synonyms in the sentences we read today. They help us build a strong vocabulary for writing! For instance, consider the example of Synonyms: large/big, small/tiny, hot/warm To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Synonyms
examples.content: large/big, small/tiny, hot/warm

slides.title: Synonyms in Nature
slides.text: Let's look at some synonyms we use in science. 'Small' and 'tiny' (used to describe garden insects). 'Warm' and 'hot' (used to describe solar heat). 'Wet' and 'damp' (used to describe fertile soil). 'Quick' and 'fast' (used to describe animal movement). Notice how both words mean almost the same thing. They help us give detailed descriptions of physical objects. Can you find a synonym for 'desk'? It is table! For instance, consider the example of Soil: The damp soil is wet. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Soil
examples.content: The damp soil is wet.

slides.title: Vocabulary: Antonyms
slides.text: Antonyms are words that have opposite meanings in text. They help us compare different physical properties of things. E.g., the words 'hot' and 'cold' are antonyms. The words 'rough' and 'smooth' are also antonyms. Both words describe opposite states of temperature or texture. Using antonyms helps us contrast different solid materials. Let's find antonyms in the sentences we read today. They are essential for comparing physical details! For instance, consider the example of Antonyms: hot/cold, rough/smooth, hard/soft To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Antonyms
examples.content: hot/cold, rough/smooth, hard/soft

slides.title: Antonyms in Materials
slides.text: Let's compare solid materials using antonyms in our logs. A diamond is hard, but a cotton ball is soft. A glass mirror is shiny, but a charcoal block is dull. The words 'hard' and 'soft' are antonyms. The words 'shiny' and 'dull' are antonyms too. They show how different materials have opposite properties. These contrasts help us classify objects in science lessons. Can you name an antonym for 'wet'? Yes, it is dry! For instance, consider the example of Comparison: Metal is shiny, but wood is dull. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Comparison
examples.content: Metal is shiny, but wood is dull.

slides.title: Science Connection: Weather Predictions
slides.text: Meteorologists are scientists who predict the weather daily. They use tools like thermometers to measure air heat. They observe cloud patterns and wind direction in the sky. Then, they make predictions about rain or sunshine. Their predictions help us decide if we need an umbrella. They use logic and evidence to keep our community safe. We can practice being weather scientists in our writing too. Let's write a weather prediction in our journal today! For instance, consider the example of Observation: Using a thermometer to measure heat. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Observation
examples.content: Using a thermometer to measure heat.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Spotting Adjectives
slides.text: Let's play a game to spot describing words. Read: 'The shiny metal nail is wet with rain.' Which words describe the physical properties of the nail? Yes, the adjectives are 'shiny' and 'wet'! They tell us how the nail looks and feels. The other words are nouns, articles, or verbs. Always look for words that describe the noun's state. What adjective describes a rock that does not bend? For instance, consider the example of Adjectives: shiny (property of nail), wet (state of nail) To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Adjectives
examples.content: shiny (property of nail), wet (state of nail)

slides.title: Guided Practice: Sentence Type
slides.text: Let's practice identifying different types of sentences. Read this: 'Do plants need soil to grow?' What type of sentence is this in grammar? It ends with a question mark, so it is interrogative! If it were: 'Plants need soil to grow.', it is declarative. If it were: 'Please put soil in the pot.', it is imperative. Checking the punctuation mark is the easiest clue of all. What type of sentence ends with a question mark? For instance, consider the example of Check: Interrogative because it asks a question. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Check
examples.content: Interrogative because it asks a question.

slides.title: Guided Practice: Synonyms
slides.text: Let's practice finding synonyms in our vocabulary list. What is a synonym for the word 'silent'? A. loud, B. quiet, C. red, D. hard? Yes, the correct answer is B! Silent means quiet. Both words describe a state of no sound vibrations. Using synonyms makes your sentences sound very natural. Can you find a synonym for 'quick' in your mind? Yes, the synonym is 'fast'! Great job! For instance, consider the example of Synonym Check: silent = quiet To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Synonym Check
examples.content: silent = quiet

slides.title: Common Error: Describing Opinions
slides.text: A common mistake is using opinion adjectives in science. Do not write: 'This is a beautiful green leaf.' 'Beautiful' is a personal opinion, not a scientific fact. Instead, write: 'This is a green leaf with five points.' 'Green' and 'five points' are factual observations using senses. Factual adjectives can be measured and tested by anyone. Always use factual describing words in your science logs. They keep your descriptions objective and scientifically accurate! For instance, consider the example of Opinion (Avoid): This is a pretty flower. For instance, consider the example of Fact (Prefer): This is a yellow flower. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Opinion (Avoid)
examples.content: This is a pretty flower.

examples.title: Fact (Prefer)
examples.content: This is a yellow flower.

slides.title: Summary of Week 4
slides.text: Congratulations! You completed all the slides for Week 4. Let's review the main language tools we mastered. We reviewed CVCC, CCVC, and VCV word spelling patterns. We learned to use adjectives to describe solid properties. We reviewed declarative, interrogative, and imperative sentences. We learned to make logical predictions based on clues. We learned to compare words using synonyms and antonyms. You are now ready to take the quiz and challenges! For instance, consider the example of Review list: Spelling, Adjectives, Sentence types, Synonyms/Antonyms. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Review list
examples.content: Spelling, Adjectives, Sentence types, Synonyms/Antonyms.

Challenge: A student predicts: 'A dry leaf will sink in water.' Is this a logical prediction?
options: No, because leaves are light and float.
