slides.title: Buod ng Ikaapat na Linggo
slides.text: Binabati kita! Natapos mo ang lahat ng slide sa linggong ito. Natutunan natin ang matalinong pamumuhay sa ating tahanan. Nalaman natin ang pagtitipid sa pagkain at pamimili doon. Nag-recycle tayo ng plastik na bote upang gawing paso. Nag-impok tayo ng barya at nagtipid ng papel at lapis natin. Nalaman natin ang ugnayan sa pagitan ng tipid at badyet. Handa ka na ba sa pagsusulit at hamon ng yunit ngayon? Ipakita ang iyong disiplina at galing sa GMRC ngayon! Halimbawa, tingnan natin ang tungkol sa Tandaan: Tipid = Matalinong pamumuhay. Recycle = Bawas-gastos. Badyet = Disiplina. Upang maunawaan ang konseptong ito nang malalim, kailangan nating tingnan kung paano ito nalalapat sa iba't ibang sitwasyon sa ating pang-araw-araw na buhay. Sa pamamagitan ng pag-aaral ng paksang ito, ang mga mag-aaral ay maaaring bumuo ng mga kasanayan sa kritikal na pag-iisip.

examples.title: Tandaan
examples.content: Tipid = Matalinong pamumuhay. Recycle = Bawas-gastos. Badyet = Disiplina.

standard.q: Ano ang tawag sa matalino at disiplinadong paraan ng pamumuhay sa tahanan?
options: matalinong pamumuhay o pagtitipid

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang isang pangunahing 'kailangan' ng isang bata sa eskwela?
options: lapis at aklat

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang isang 'gusto' o luho na maaaring ipagpaliban ang pagbili?
options: bagong laruang kotse

standard.q: Ano ang dapat gawin sa mga tirang pagkain pagkatapos ng hapunan?
options: itapon sa basurahan ng kusina
itabi nang maayos sa refrigerator upang maiinit muli
iwanan sa mesa nang walang takip
ipakain sa aso ng kapitbahay

standard.q: Ano ang dapat gamitin ng nanay kapag namimili sa palengke upang maiwasan ang labis na gastos?
options: shopping list o listahan ng bibilhin
malaking bag na walang laman

standard.q: Paano mo titipirin ang pagsulat sa iyong notebook sa paaralan?
options: punitin ang mga pahina kapag nagkamali
gamitin ang likod ng mga lumang papel para sa scratch
sumulat nang malalaking letra upang maubos agad ang papel
bumili ng bagong notebook araw-araw

standard.q: Ano ang angkop na gawin sa nabaling dulo ng iyong lapis?
options: itapon ang lapis sa basurahan
itasa ito gamit ang sharpener upang magamit muli
hingi ng bagong lapis sa guro
itago sa ilalim ng desk

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang halimbawa ng pag-recycle ng plastik na bote ng gatas?
options: itapon sa ilog ng barangay
gupitin at gawing paso para sa halaman sa hardin
sunugin sa bakuran
gawing laruan ng aso

standard.q: Sino ang makakatipid sa pamilya kapag pinatay mo ang ilaw kapag umaalis sa silid?
options: ang kapitan ng barangay lamang
ang iyong mga magulang sa babayarang bill
ang mga tanod sa outpost

standard.q: Tama o Mali: Ang pag-recycle ng mga lata bilang alkansya ay nagpapakita ng matalinong pagtitipid.
Ano ang pinakamahusay na paraan upang magplano ng iyong badyet sa baon na 20 pesos?
options: gastusin ang lahat sa kendi
itabi ang 5 pesos sa alkansya at gamitin ang 15 pesos sa sapat na pagkain
itapon ang barya sa kalsada
manghingi pa ng 50 pesos

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang pambalana sa pook kung saan tayo namimili ng gulay at isda?
Anong yunit ng enerhiya ang binabayaran ng pamilya buwan-buwan na ginagamit para sa bentilador?
Punan ang patlang: 'Ang pag-iwas sa pagsasayang ng pagkain ay nagpapakita ng ___ sa mga magsasaka.'
options: pagpapahalaga at paggalang

standard.q: Ano ang dapat gawin sa mga papel na may sulat sa harap ngunit malinis pa ang likod?
options: gawing eroplanong papel at itapon
gamiting scratch paper sa pagkuwenta sa math
sunugin sa bakuran
itapon sa drum ng tubig

standard.q: Saan galing ang mga hilaw na materyal sa paggawa ng papel na ating sinusulatan?
options: sa mga plastik na bote
sa mga puno sa kagubatan
sa mga metal na susi
sa semento ng pader

standard.q: Tama o Mali: Ang pagbili ng bagong bag kahit maayos pa ang luma ay isang halimbawa ng pagtitipid.
Aling ugali ang ipinapakita ng batang nagpapaalala sa kanyang ama na patayin ang TV kapag matutulog na?
options: malasakit at pagiging matipid sa pamilya

standard.q: Ano ang angkop na gamitin upang patubuin ang isang seedling sa klase ng science?
options: recycled na bote ng gatas na ginawang paso

standard.q: Ano ang pangunahing benepisyo ng pag-recycle ng mga lata at bote sa bahay?
options: makabili ng bagong sapatos
mabawasan ang basura at makatipid sa pagbili ng mga bagong kagamitan
maging sikat sa plaza
makasali sa parade ng lechon

standard.q: Alin sa mga sumusunod ang tamang kilos kapag nakita mong bukas ang ilaw sa silid kahit walang tao?
options: hayaang nakabukas upang maging maliwanag
patayin ang ilaw nang kusa upang makatipid sa kuryente
isumbong sa pulis outpost
tawanan ang nakalimot

standard.q: Tukuyin ang pangkalahatang bagay na ginagamit sa pagtatabi ng barya sa inyong kuwarto:
Tama o Mali: Ang pag-iimpok ng barya sa alkansyang kawayan ay tradisyon na ipinasa ng ating mga ninuno.
Alin sa mga sumusunod ang nagpapakita ng tamang pagtitipid sa tubig sa inyong tahanan?
options: hayaang nakabukas ang gripo habang nagsasabon ng pinggan
gumamit ng balde at baso at isara ang gripo kapag tapos na
maglaro ng hose sa bakuran
itapon ang tubig sa kalsada

standard.q: Saan natin dapat ilapat ang ating mga natutunang matalinong pagtitipid sa GMRC?
options: sa paaralan lamang tuwing may guro
sa tahanan kasama ang pamilya sa araw-araw na pamumuhay
tuwing pista ng bayan lamang
kapag binibigyan ng baon lamang

challenge.q: Challenge: Kung ang isang mag-aaral ay nais makatulong sa pagbawas ng utility bills ng kanyang pamilya, aling kilos ang pinakamahusay?
options: magpatakbo ng bentilador buong araw kahit malamig ang panahon
kusang patayin ang mga ilaw at bentilador kapag walang tao sa silid at isara ang gripo nang mahigpit
maglaro ng computer habang nakabukas ang TV
huwag gumamit ng tubig kailanman sa bahay

challenge.q: Challenge: Paano nagkakaugnay ang pagtitipid ng papel sa pag-aalaga ng mga puno sa kagubatan?
options: Dahil ang papel ay yari sa kahoy ng puno, kaya ang pagtitipid ng papel ay nagpapababa sa pangangailangang putulin ang mga puno.
Walang ugnayan ang papel sa mga puno sa gubat.
Ang pagtitipid ng papel ay nagpaparami ng mga plastik na bote.
Ang pagtitipid ng papel ay nagpapasikat sa mga historyador.

challenge.q: Challenge: Kung ang nanay ni Matteo ay may listahan ng pamimili ngunit bumili pa rin siya ng tatlong mamahaling laruan na labas sa badyet, ano ang epekto nito?
options: Mas makakatipid ang pamilya sa pagkain.
Mababawasan ang pera para sa mga pangunahing kailangan tulad ng bigas at gamot.
Magiging masaya ang kapitan ng barangay.
Walang magiging epekto sa badyet ng pamilya.

challenge.q: Challenge: Bakit ang paggamit ng likod ng lumang sulat bilang scratch paper ay nagpapakita ng matalinong pamumuhay ng isang Grade 3 student?
options: Dahil iniiwasan nitong masayang ang mga blankong pahina ng bagong notebook na inilaan sa klase.
Dahil mas madali itong sulatan kaysa sa pader.
Dahil binabayaran siya ng kanyang guro sa paggamit nito.
Dahil ang lumang sulat ay may gintong barya sa likod.

challenge.q: Challenge: Aling ugnayan ang pinakatumpak sa pagitan ng pag-recycle ng bote at pagtitipid sa badyet ng pamilya?
options: Nag-recycle ng bote upang ibenta sa palengke ng bansa.
Gumawa ng paso gamit ang bote upang hindi na kailangang bumili ng bagong paso sa tindahan, na bawas-gastos sa magulang.
Hindi nag-recycle dahil mas maganda ang bumili ng bagong paso.
Gumawa ng alkansya upang itapon sa ilog.

performance.title: Tsart ng Pag-recycle ng Pamilya
performance.desc: Gumawa ng isang recycled na kagamitan sa inyong bahay (tulad ng paso mula sa bote ng gatas, o alkansya mula sa lata). Ipakita ang iyong nagawa sa iyong magulang. Sumulat ng maikling paliwanag sa papel kung paano nakakatipid ang pag-recycle na ito sa badyet ng inyong pamilya. Talakayin ang ulat na ito nang may tiwala sa harap ng iyong pamilya.
labels: Wastong nakagawa ng isang recycled na kagamitan mula sa bote o lata
Maayos na naisulat ang paliwanag tungkol sa pagtitipid sa badyet
Malinaw at may lakas ng loob na naipaliwanag ang ulat sa pamilya

pages:
Pagsasanay 1: Kailangan vs. Gusto sa Pamumuhay

Tukuyin kung ang sumusunod na bagay ay Kailangan (essential) o Gusto (luho/hindi kailangan) gamit ang stylus.

1. bigas para sa pamilya
Kailangan
2. bagong laruang robot
Gusto
3. gamot para sa may-sakit
Kailangan

Pagsasanay 2: Pagsusuri ng Scratch Paper

Isulat kung ang gawi ay Matipid (karapat-dapat) o Matalak (dapat iwasan/pagsasayang ng papel).

1. Pagsusulat ng scratch sa likod ng mga lumang sulat na malinis pa.
Matipid
2. Punitin ang mga blankong pahina ng bagong notebook para gawing scratch paper.
Matalak

Pagsasanay 3: Pagsusuri ng Pamimili

Sagutin ang tanong gamit ang tumpak na detalye batay sa matalinong pamimili.

Bakit kailangang gumawa ng listahan ng bibilhin ang nanay bago pumunta sa palengke ng bayan?
Upang bilhin lamang ang mga kailangan at maiwasan ang paggastos sa mga hindi kailangang bagay.
Nakatutulong ito upang makatipid sa badyet ng pamilya at makapag-impok ng barya.

slides.title: Story: The Frog Prince - Part 1
slides.text: Once upon a time, in a grand kingdom far away, lived a young princess who was incredibly beautiful. Her favorite toy was a heavy golden ball. On hot afternoons, she loved to sit by a deep, cool well in the palace gardens, tossing the golden ball high into the air and catching it. One sunny day, she threw the ball too high. It slipped through her fingers, bounced off the stone rim, and fell directly into the dark, deep water of the well. The well was so deep that she could not see the bottom. The princess sat by the well and began to cry loudly. 'Oh dear, I will do anything to get my golden ball back!' she cried. Suddenly, an ugly frog popped his head out of the green water. 'Why are you crying, princess?' croaked the frog. She explained her problem, and the frog made a proposal. 'I will retrieve your ball if you promise to let me be your companion, eat from your golden plate, and sleep in your cozy bed.' The princess, eager to get her toy, quickly agreed, thinking the frog was silly and could never leave his well.

slides.title: Story: The Frog Prince - Part 2
slides.text: The frog dove deep into the cold water and soon returned with the golden ball in his mouth. He dropped it on the grass, and the princess, filled with joy, grabbed her toy and ran back to the palace without thanking him. The poor frog croaked after her, but she ignored him completely. That evening, as the princess sat at the dining table with the king and her sisters, they heard a strange tapping sound at the palace door. 'Princess, open the door and let me in!' called a small voice. The princess opened the door, saw the ugly frog, and shut it in fear. The king noticed her worry and asked what was wrong. After she explained, the king said firmly, 'A promise is a promise. You must keep your word. Let the frog in and share your dinner with him.' The princess reluctantly let the frog sit on the table and eat from her plate, though it made her feel very uncomfortable.

slides.title: Story: The Frog Prince - Part 3
slides.text: After dinner, the frog said, 'I am tired. Please carry me to your room so I can sleep in your bed.' The princess refused and cried, but the king insisted she keep her promise. She carried the frog upstairs and placed him in the corner. But the frog hopped onto her pillow, demanding to sleep next to her. In anger, the princess picked up the frog and threw him gently against the wall. The moment the frog touched the wall, he transformed into a handsome prince! He explained that an evil witch had cursed him, and only a princess's companion could break it. With the king's blessing, they became best friends and eventually ruled the kingdom together, proving that keeping promises and looking beyond appearance leads to true happiness.

slides.title: Vocabulary & Word Study
slides.text:
Vocabulary:
• Retrieve - To get or bring something back from a place.
• Proposal - A plan or suggestion put forward for consideration.
• Reluctantly - In an unwilling and hesitant way.

Phonics Focus: Practice the /pr/ blend in 'prince', 'princess', and 'promise'.

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. What was the princess's favorite toy, and how did she lose it?
2. What did the frog ask in return for retrieving the ball?
3. Why did the king force the princess to let the frog eat from her plate?

slides.title: Moral Lesson & Reflection
slides.text:
Moral Focus: Honesty and keeping promises.

We must always keep our promises, even when it is difficult or inconvenient. True character is shown by holding onto our word and not judging others solely by their appearance.

Discuss: Tell about a time you made a promise. Did you keep it?

slides.title: Story: Rapunzel - Part 1
slides.text: A long time ago, a husband and wife lived next to a beautiful walled garden owned by a powerful witch named Dame Gothel. The wife fell ill and longed for the fresh green rampion (a salad herb) growing in the witch's garden. The husband, desperate to save his wife, climbed the high wall at night to steal some rampion. But on his second trip, the witch caught him. 'How dare you steal from me!' she screeched. The husband begged for mercy, explaining his wife's illness. The witch agreed to let him take the rampion under one condition: they must give her their firstborn child. Terrified, the husband agreed. When a beautiful baby girl was born, the witch took her away and named her Rapunzel. As Rapunzel grew into a lovely girl with incredibly long, golden hair that shone like spun gold, the witch locked her in a tall tower in the middle of a deep forest. The tower had no doors or stairs, only a single window at the very top.

slides.title: Story: Rapunzel - Part 2
slides.text: Whenever Dame Gothel wanted to enter the tower, she stood at the bottom and called out: 'Rapunzel, Rapunzel, let down your hair!' Hearing the call, Rapunzel would unwind her long, braided golden hair, loop it over a hook by the window, and let it drop seventy feet to the ground. The witch would then climb up using the hair as a rope. Rapunzel lived a lonely life in the tower, spending her days singing beautiful songs to pass the time. One day, a young prince riding through the forest heard her sweet voice and was enchanted. He watched the tower and saw the witch climb up. The next evening, after the witch left, the prince stood at the base of the tower and called in a disguised voice: 'Rapunzel, Rapunzel, let down your hair!' The golden hair tumbled down, and the prince climbed up.

slides.title: Story: Rapunzel - Part 3
slides.text: Rapunzel was frightened at first, but the prince was kind and gentle. They quickly became close friends and made a plan for her escape. The prince promised to bring silk threads every visit so Rapunzel could weave a ladder. However, during a visit by the witch, Rapunzel accidentally revealed the secret. In a rage, Dame Gothel cut off Rapunzel's long golden hair and cast her out into a wilderness. When the prince returned, the witch let down the cut hair, and when he reached the window, she mocked him. In despair, the prince fell from the tower into some thorn bushes, which scratched his eyes. He wandered the forest blind for years, until he heard Rapunzel's sweet voice singing. They reunited, and Rapunzel's tears of joy fell onto his eyes, restoring his sight. They returned to his kingdom and lived happily ever after.

slides.title: Vocabulary & Word Study
slides.text:
Vocabulary:
• Walled - Enclosed or surrounded by a wall.
• Enchanted - Filled with delight; captivated.
• Wilderness - A wild, uncultivated, and uninhabited region.

Phonics Focus: Practice the /l/ sounds in 'lovely', 'long', and 'golden'.

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. Why did the husband steal rampion from the witch's garden?
2. How did the witch and the prince get inside the tall tower?
3. How did the prince regain his sight at the end of the story?

slides.title: Moral Lesson & Reflection
slides.text:
Moral Focus: Love, patience, and hope through difficult times.

Even in the darkest and loneliest times, we must keep hope in our hearts. True friendship and love can overcome great obstacles and bring healing.

Discuss: What is something you do to stay happy when you feel lonely?

wednesday.title: Phonics Fun with Ch and Sh

slides.title: Tongue Twister Fun: Ch and Sh - Part 1
slides.text: Welcome to our speech playground! Today we are practicing the soft /sh/ and crisp /ch/ sounds. Meet Charlie the Chef, who loves to chop vegetables in his kitchen. Charlie chips and chops cheap cheese. He makes delicious cherry soup. Let's try saying this together slowly: 'Charlie the Chef chips and chops cheap cheese in the kitchen.' Notice how your tongue touches the roof of your mouth for the /ch/ sound. Now let's try it faster! 'Charlie chops cheap cheese. Cheap cheese Charlie chops.' Repeat it three times to train your speech muscles. This helps you pronounce words clearly when you talk to others.

slides.title: Tongue Twister Fun: Ch and Sh - Part 2
slides.text: Now, let's practice the /sh/ sound. Meet Sally, who sells sea shells by the sea shore. The shells she sells are surely sea shells. Sally's sister, Sharon, washes shiny shoes in the shop. Sharon shines six shiny shoes. Let's try saying: 'Sally sells sea shells by the sea shore. Sharon shines shiny shoes.' For the /sh/ sound, blow air out gently without letting your tongue touch the roof of your mouth. Practice this difference: 'chips' vs 'ships', 'cheat' vs 'sheet', 'chop' vs 'shop'. This is an excellent way to train your ears and tongue.

slides.title: Tongue Twister Fun: Ch and Sh - Part 3
slides.text: Let's combine /ch/ and /sh/ for the ultimate phonics challenge! Imagine Charlie the Chef cooking on a big ship. 'Charlie the Chef chose shiny shells to decorate his ship kitchen.' Let's try saying this fast: 'Charlie chose shiny shells. Shiny shells Charlie chose.' Repeat it three times: slow, medium, and fast! Congratulations, you are practicing clear speech. This helps you sound confident and clear when you speak at home or at school.

slides.title: Speech & Enunciation Practice
slides.text:
Phonics Focus: Contrasting /ch/ and /sh/
• /ch/ (Stop-Fricative): Touch tongue to roof, then release air quickly (e.g., 'choose', 'chair').
• /sh/ (Continuous Fricative): Keep tongue down, blow air continuously (e.g., 'shoe', 'shine').

Daily enunciation practice builds reading confidence!

slides.title: Comprehension & Speech Tasks
slides.text:
Tasks:
1. Read: 'Sharon shines cheap shoes.' without mixing up the /sh/ and /ch/ sounds.
2. List three words starting with /ch/ and three starting with /sh/ from the slides.
3. Practice saying 'cheap sheep' five times fast.

slides.title: Focus: Perseverance & Speech Confidence
slides.text:
Speech exercises help us express our thoughts clearly. It is okay if you make mistakes—just pause, take a deep breath, and try again.

Discuss: When is it important to speak slowly and clearly?

thursday.title: The Goose that Laid the Golden Eggs

slides.title: Story: The Goose that Laid the Golden Eggs - Part 1
slides.text: Once upon a time, a poor farmer and his wife lived in a small, humble cottage. They worked hard every day planting vegetables and caring for their few farm animals, but they barely made enough money to buy food. One morning, the farmer went to the nest of his favorite goose and found a heavy, shiny yellow egg. To his astonishment, the egg was made of pure, solid gold! He ran to the village and sold it for a large sum of money. The next morning, the goose laid another golden egg. The farmer could hardly believe his luck. Every single morning, without fail, the goose laid one shiny golden egg. Within a few months, the poor farmer and his wife became incredibly wealthy, building a huge stone house and buying fine clothes.

slides.title: Story: The Goose that Laid the Golden Eggs - Part 2
slides.text: As the farmer and his wife grew richer, they also became very greedy. They were no longer satisfied with getting just one golden egg a day. They wanted to buy more land and horses immediately. 'Why should we wait for just one egg each morning?' the farmer's wife said one evening. 'The goose must be filled with gold inside. If we cut it open, we can get all the gold at once and become the richest people in the world tonight!' The farmer, blinded by his greed, agreed with his wife's plan. They went to the coop, caught the goose, and killed it. However, when they cut the goose open, they were shocked to find that its inside was just like any other ordinary goose. There was no gold at all inside it.

slides.title: Story: The Goose that Laid the Golden Eggs - Part 3
slides.text: The farmer and his wife realized their terrible mistake. In their greed to get everything at once, they had lost the precious goose that had made them rich. Now, they would never get another golden egg. They sat in their wealthy home and wept, regretting their foolish decision. This story shows that greed can make us lose the good things we already have. Being content with steady progress is far better than trying to get everything instantly through greed.

slides.title: Vocabulary & Word Study
slides.text:
Vocabulary:
• Astonishment - Great surprise or amazement.
• Greedy - Having an excessive desire for wealth or food.
• Content - Satisfied with what one has.

Phonics Focus: Practice the /g/ sound in 'goose', 'gold', and 'greedy'.

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. What did the farmer find in the goose's nest on the first morning?
2. Why did the farmer and his wife decide to kill the goose?
3. What did they find when they cut the goose open?

slides.title: Moral Lesson & Reflection
slides.text:
Moral Focus: Greed and Contentment.

Greed often overrides wisdom and results in losing what we have. We should appreciate steady blessings and avoid rushing to acquire wealth at the expense of others or common sense.

Discuss: What is the difference between wanting to improve and being greedy?

slides.title: Story: The Emperor's New Clothes - Part 1
slides.text: Many years ago, there lived an Emperor who cared more about fine clothes than about running his empire. He spent all his money on new suits and changed his outfits every hour. One day, two clever swindlers arrived in the city, claiming to be weavers. They declared they could weave a magical fabric that was invisible to anyone who was either stupid or unfit for their job. The Emperor, thinking this was a wonderful way to find out who was wise in his court, paid the swindlers a large sum of gold to make him a new suit of clothes immediately. The swindlers set up two looms and pretended to work, although there was nothing on the looms at all.

slides.title: Story: The Emperor's New Clothes - Part 2
slides.text: The Emperor sent his honest old minister to check on the weavers' progress. When the minister saw the empty looms, he was shocked, but he thought, 'Oh dear, if I say I see nothing, they will think I am stupid or unfit for my post!' So he pretended to see the beautiful patterns and reported this to the Emperor. Later, the Emperor went himself, accompanied by his courtiers. None of them could see anything, but they all feared being called foolish, so they praised the invisible cloth. 'It is magnificent!' they all said. The swindlers worked through the night, pretending to stitch the invisible suit, and finally presented it to the Emperor.

slides.title: Story: The Emperor's New Clothes - Part 3
slides.text: The Emperor took off his old clothes, and the swindlers pretended to dress him in the new suit. The Emperor marched in a grand procession through the city. The citizens, not wanting to be labeled stupid, cried, 'How beautiful the Emperor's new clothes are!' Suddenly, a little child in the crowd pointed and said: 'But he has nothing on!' The words spread, and soon the entire crowd began to whisper and laugh, shouting, 'He has no clothes on!' The Emperor realized they were right, but he held his head high and continued the procession, feeling very foolish for letting his vanity blind him to the truth.

slides.title: Vocabulary & Word Study
slides.text:
Vocabulary:
• Swindlers - People who use deception to steal money.
• Courtiers - Attendants at a royal court.
• Procession - A number of people moving forward in an orderly, formal way.

Phonics Focus: Practice the /c/ sound in 'clothes', 'clever', and 'court'.

slides.title: Comprehension Questions
slides.text:
Questions:
1. What was the Emperor's main interest in life?
2. What was special about the magical fabric according to the swindlers?
3. Who was the first person to speak the truth during the procession?

slides.title: Moral Lesson & Reflection
slides.text:
Moral Focus: Honesty and avoiding vanity.

We should speak the truth honestly, even when others are afraid to do so. Pride and fear of what others think can make us blind to simple truths.

Discuss: Why do you think the little child was the only one who spoke the truth?

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Leon at ang Daga - Bahagi 1
slides.text: Isang mainit na tanghali, isang malaking Leon ang natutulog sa kanyang yungib. Isang maliit na Daga ang naglalaro sa malapit at aksidenteng nakatakbo sa ibabaw ng mukha ng Leon. Nagising ang Leon na may malakas na ungal, at hinuli ang Daga gamit ang kanyang malaking paw. 'Patawad, Haring Leon!' umiiyak na pakiusap ng Daga. 'Huwag mo akong kainin. Isang araw ay tutulungan kita bilang ganti.' Natawa ang Leon sa ideya na ang isang maliit na daga ay makakatulong sa isang hari. Ngunit dahil nasa mabuting mood siya, pinakawalan niya ang Daga.

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Leon at ang Daga - Bahagi 2
slides.text: Makalipas ang ilang araw, habang naghahanap ng makakain sa kagubatan ang Leon, nasakote siya sa lambat ng mga mangangaso. Nagpupumiglas siya ngunit lalo lamang humihigpit ang mga lubid. Sumigaw ang Leon sa galit at takot, at narinig ito ng maliit na Daga. Agad na tumakbo ang Daga sa kinaroroonan ng Leon. 'Huwag kang mag-alala, Haring Leon, ililigtas kita!' sabi ng Daga. Gamit ang kanyang matatalas na ngipin, sinimulan niyang ngatngatin ang makapal na lubid ng lambat.

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Leon at ang Daga - Bahagi 3
slides.text: Hindi sumuko ang Daga kahit nangangalay na ang kanyang panga. Nginatngat niya ang lubid hanggang sa maputol ang pangunahing buhol nito at lumuwag ang lambat. Nakawala ang Leon at nakaligtas! Lumingon ang Leon sa Daga at sinabi, 'Salamat, maliit na kaibigan. Nagkamali ako nang tawanan ko ang iyong pangako.' Mula noon, naging matalik silang magkaibigan, nagtutulungan sa lahat ng bagay sa kagubatan, na nagpapatunay na walang kaibigan na sobrang liit upang makatulong sa iba.

slides.title: Talasalitaan at Pag-aaral ng Salita
slides.text: Mga Bokabularyo: • Yungib - Isang yungib o kuweba na tirahan ng mga hayop. • Nasakote - Nahuli sa bitag o trap. • Ngatngatin - Kagatin o sirain ang isang bagay gamit ang matatalas na ngipin. Tutok sa Phonics: Magsanay sa pagbigkas ng tunog ng 'd' sa 'daga', 'dulo', at 'dapit-hapon'.

slides.title: Mga Tanong sa Pag-unawa
slides.text: Mga Tanong: 1. Bakit natawa ang Leon sa sinabi ng Daga noong una silang magkita? 2. Paano nahuli ang Leon ng mga mangangaso? 3. Paano tinulungan ng Daga ang Leon na makalaya sa bitag?

slides.title: Mabuting Aral at Pagninilay
slides.text: Kaisipan: Ang kabaitan ay hindi kailanman nasasayang, at ang bawat isa ay may halaga. Huwag maliitin ang maliliit na nilalang o kaibigan dahil may kani-kaniyang talento at kakayahan ang bawat isa. Ang pagtulong sa nangangailangan ay nagbabalik ng kabutihan sa atin. Talakayin: Paano mo matutulungan ang isang kaibigan na mas maliit o mas bata sa iyo?

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Mahiwagang Palayok - Bahagi 1
slides.text: Sa isang maliit na nayon, nakatira ang isang mabait na batang babae na si Maya at ang kanyang matandang lola. Sila ay napakahirap at madalas ay walang makain. Isang araw, habang naghahanap ng mga tuyong kahoy si Maya sa kagubatan, nakatagpo siya ng isang matandang babae na may mabigat na pasanin. Tinulungan ito ni Maya nang walang alinlangan. Bilang pasasalamat, binigyan siya ng matanda ng isang maliit na palayok. 'Ito ay isang mahiwagang palayok. Sabihin mo lang ang "Luto, munting palayok, luto!" at magluluto ito ng matamis at masarap na lugaw. Kapag busog ka na, sabihin mo ang "Hinto, munting palayok, hinto!" at titigil ito,' paliwanag ng matanda.

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Mahiwagang Palayok - Bahagi 2
slides.text: Masayang umuwi si Maya at ipinakita sa kanyang lola ang mahiwagang palayok. Mula noon, hindi na sila nagutom dahil tuwing kakain sila ay nagluluto ang palayok ng masarap na pagkain. Isang araw, umalis si Maya upang bumisita sa kabilang baryo. Nagutom ang kanyang lola at sinabi sa palayok: 'Luto, munting palayok, luto!' Agad na nagluto ang palayok ng mainit na lugaw. Kumain nang kumain ang lola hanggang sa mabusog. Ngunit nang nais na niyang patigilin ang palayok, nakalimutan niya ang tamang salita. Sinabi niya: 'Salamat, tapos na!', 'Hinto na!' ngunit patuloy pa rin ang palayok sa pagluluto.

slides.title: Kuwento: Ang Mahiwagang Palayok - Bahagi 3
slides.text: Umapaw ang lugaw mula sa palayok, gumapang sa sahig, lumabas sa pinto, at napuno ang buong kalsada ng nayon. Nagtakbuhan ang mga kapitbahay dahil sa bumabahang lugaw. Nang bumalik si Maya at nakita ang sitwasyon, agad siyang sumigaw ng malakas: 'Hinto, munting palayok, hinto!' Agad na tumigil ang palayok sa pagluluto. Kinailangang kainin ng mga tao ang lugaw sa kalsada upang makadaan muli. Mula noon, naging maingat na ang lola at si Maya sa paggamit nito, at ibinahagi nila ang pagkain sa lahat nang may wastong disiplina.

slides.title: Talasalitaan at Pag-aaral ng Salita
slides.text: Mga Bokabularyo: • Nayon - Isang maliit na komunidad o pamayanan sa probinsya. • Alinlangan - Pag-aalinlangan o takot na tumulong. • Umapaw - Lumabas ang likido dahil napuno ang lalagyan. Tutok sa Phonics: Magsanay sa pagbigkas ng tunog ng 'y' sa 'palayok', 'nayon', at 'maya'.

slides.title: Mga Tanong sa Pag-unawa
slides.text: Mga Tanong: 1. Anong mahiwagang salita ang nagpapaluto sa palayok? 2. Bakit umapaw ang lugaw at bumaha sa buong nayon? 3. Paano pinatigil ni Maya ang pag-apaw ng lugaw?

slides.title: Mabuting Aral at Pagninilay
slides.text: Kaisipan: Ang bawat biyaya ay dapat gamitin nang may wastong kaalaman at disiplina. Mahalagang makinig nang mabuti sa mga panuto at alalahanin ang mga ito upang maiwasan ang sakuna. Ang pagbabahagi ng biyaya sa kapwa ay nagdadala ng saya sa komunidad. Talakayin: Bakit mahalagang makinig at sumunod sa mga panuto ng guro sa paaralan?

slides.title: Exploring Large Numbers
slides.text: Let's learn about whole numbers up to ten thousand. These numbers have four digits and help us count large collections of objects in daily life. In math, we express this as: Numbers up to 10,000 = big values. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Can you think of a real-world collection that has thousands of items?

examples.title: Example 1
examples.content: Review standard details: Numbers up to 10,000 = big values.

slides.title: Visualizing Thousands
slides.text: We can visualize numbers using base-ten blocks. A large block represents one thousand, which is made of ten flat hundreds blocks joined together. In math, we express this as: 1000 = 10 hundreds blocks. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. How many tens blocks do we need to make one thousand blocks?

examples.title: Example 2
examples.content: Review standard details: 1000 = 10 hundreds blocks.

slides.title: The Four-Digit Place Value Chart
slides.text: We organize four-digit numbers in a place value chart. The positions from right to left are Ones, Tens, Hundreds, and Thousands. In math, we express this as: Place values = Thousands, Hundreds, Tens, Ones. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Which digit in the number 4,567 is in the thousands place?

examples.title: Example 3
examples.content: Review standard details: Place values = Thousands, Hundreds, Tens, Ones.

slides.title: Understanding Place Value
slides.text: Place value is the position of a digit in a number. It tells us if the digit represents ones, tens, hundreds, or thousands. In math, we express this as: Place value of 5 in 5,432 = Thousands. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. What is the place value of the digit 3 in the number 8,312?

examples.title: Example 4
examples.content: Review standard details: Place value of 5 in 5,432 = Thousands.

slides.title: Calculating the Value of a Digit
slides.text: The value of a digit is found by multiplying the digit by its place value. For example, a 6 in the hundreds place has a value of 600. In math, we express this as: Value of 6 in 7,621 = 6 x 100 = 600. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. What is the value of the digit 4 in the number 4,921?

examples.title: Example 5
examples.content: Review standard details: Value of 6 in 7,621 = 6 x 100 = 600.

slides.title: Reading Numbers in Words
slides.text: When reading a number, we read the thousands place, say the word thousand, and then read the remaining three digits as a standard number. In math, we express this as: 4,520 = four thousand, five hundred twenty. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. How do you read the number 7,809 in words aloud?

examples.title: Example 6
examples.content: Review standard details: 4,520 = four thousand, five hundred twenty.

slides.title: Writing Numbers in Numerals
slides.text: When writing a number, we write the digits in their correct place value positions. We use a comma to separate the thousands from the hundreds. In math, we express this as: Three thousand, forty = 3,040. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. How do you write six thousand, five hundred twelve in numerals?

examples.title: Example 7
examples.content: Review standard details: Three thousand, forty = 3,040.

slides.title: The Expanded Form
slides.text: Expanded form shows a number written as the sum of the values of its digits. This helps us see the breakdown of thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. In math, we express this as: 5,234 = 5,000 + 200 + 30 + 4. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Can you write the number 8,740 in expanded form?

examples.title: Example 8
examples.content: Review standard details: 5,234 = 5,000 + 200 + 30 + 4.

slides.title: Pineapple Harvesting counts
slides.text: Farmers in Polomolok count their harvested sweet pineapples. A small farm plot can produce four thousand, five hundred pineapples in one week. In math, we express this as: Harvest = 4,500 pineapples in Polomolok. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. If a farmer harvests 4,500 pineapples, how many thousands are there?

examples.title: Example 9
examples.content: Review standard details: Harvest = 4,500 pineapples in Polomolok.

slides.title: Lake Sebu Tourist Tracking
slides.text: The tourism office in Lake Sebu tracks the number of visitors. Last month, eight thousand, one hundred twenty-five tourists visited the scenic waterfalls. In math, we express this as: Visitors = 8,125 tourists at waterfalls. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. How do you write the tourist number 8,125 in words?

examples.title: Example 10
examples.content: Review standard details: Visitors = 8,125 tourists at waterfalls.

slides.title: Barangay Population Records
slides.text: Barangay records in South Cotabato help us track resident counts. A local barangay has nine thousand, four hundred thirty residents living in the community. In math, we express this as: Population = 9,430 residents in South Cotabato. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. What is the value of the digit 9 in the population 9,430?

examples.title: Example 11
examples.content: Review standard details: Population = 9,430 residents in South Cotabato.

slides.title: What is Rounding?
slides.text: Rounding is finding a simpler number close to the actual value. It makes numbers easier to use and estimate in daily math problems. In math, we express this as: Rounding = finding the nearest benchmark number. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Why do we round numbers when estimating total costs?

examples.title: Example 12
examples.content: Review standard details: Rounding = finding the nearest benchmark number.

slides.title: Rounding to the Nearest Ten
slides.text: To round to the nearest ten, we look at the ones digit. If it is 5 or more, we round up; if it is less than 5, we round down. In math, we express this as: 4,527 rounded to tens = 4,530. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. What is 3,412 rounded to the nearest ten?

examples.title: Example 13
examples.content: Review standard details: 4,527 rounded to tens = 4,530.

slides.title: Rounding to the Nearest Hundred
slides.text: To round to the nearest hundred, we check the tens digit. Since 8 is 5 or more, we round the hundreds digit up to the next hundred. In math, we express this as: 6,781 rounded to hundreds = 6,800. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. What is 5,230 rounded to the nearest hundred?

examples.title: Example 14
examples.content: Review standard details: 6,781 rounded to hundreds = 6,800.

slides.title: Rounding to the Nearest Thousand
slides.text: To round to the nearest thousand, we check the hundreds digit. Since 2 is less than 5, we round down, keeping the thousands digit unchanged. In math, we express this as: 8,245 rounded to thousands = 8,000. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. What is 7,850 rounded to the nearest thousand?

examples.title: Example 15
examples.content: Review standard details: 8,245 rounded to thousands = 8,000.

slides.title: Comparing Four-Digit Numbers
slides.text: To compare two numbers, we look at the digits from left to right. We start with the thousands, then hundreds, tens, and ones. In math, we express this as: 8,450 vs 8,290: 8,450 is larger. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Which number is larger: 5,670 or 5,690?

examples.title: Example 16
examples.content: Review standard details: 8,450 vs 8,290: 8,450 is larger.

slides.title: Using Comparison Symbols
slides.text: We use relation symbols to show comparison: greater than (>), less than (<), and equal to (=). The open side of the symbol points to the larger number. In math, we express this as: 4,560 > 4,520. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. If the checking digit is five or more, we round up; if it is less than five, we round down. Which symbol fits in the blank: 8,901 ___ 8,910?

examples.title: Example 17
examples.content: Review standard details: 4,560 > 4,520.

slides.title: Ordering Numbers: Smallest to Largest
slides.text: Ordering in ascending order means listing numbers from the lowest value to the highest. We compare each number to find its correct position. In math, we express this as: 3,120 < 3,450 < 3,890. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Arrange these numbers in ascending order: 4,500, 4,200, 4,800.

examples.title: Example 18
examples.content: Review standard details: 3,120 < 3,450 < 3,890.

slides.title: Ordering Numbers: Largest to Smallest
slides.text: Ordering in descending order means listing numbers from the highest value to the lowest. We find the largest number first and write them down. In math, we express this as: 9,800 > 9,500 > 9,100. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Arrange these numbers in descending order: 6,100, 6,700, 6,400.

examples.title: Example 19
examples.content: Review standard details: 9,800 > 9,500 > 9,100.

slides.title: Finding Number Patterns
slides.text: We can count in patterns of tens, hundreds, or thousands. Recognizing patterns helps us count large quantities of items very quickly. In math, we express this as: Pattern: 1,000, 2,000, 3,000, 4,000. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. What is the next number in the pattern: 3,500, 4,500, 5,500?

examples.title: Example 20
examples.content: Review standard details: Pattern: 1,000, 2,000, 3,000, 4,000.

slides.title: Zero as a Placeholder
slides.text: Zero is a vital placeholder in our number system. It shows that a place value has no value, keeping other digits in their correct positions. In math, we express this as: 4,050 vs 4,500. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Why would a number change if we removed the zero placeholder?

examples.title: Example 21
examples.content: Review standard details: 4,050 vs 4,500.

slides.title: Counting Philippine Pesos
slides.text: We use large numbers when counting money. If you save five ₱1,000 bills, four ₱100 bills, and one ₱50 bill, you have ₱5,450. In math, we express this as: Money = ₱5,450 total savings. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. How do you write five thousand, four hundred fifty Pesos in numerals?

examples.title: Example 22
examples.content: Review standard details: Money = ₱5,450 total savings.

slides.title: Common Error: Writing Placeholders
slides.text: A common error is forgetting zeros when writing numbers. For example, writing five thousand, three as 53 instead of the correct 5,003. In math, we express this as: Five thousand, three = 5,003. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Why is it incorrect to write five thousand, three as 53?

examples.title: Example 23
examples.content: Review standard details: Five thousand, three = 5,003.

slides.title: Rounding and Comparing Problems
slides.text: Let's solve a problem. A school has 4,520 students. Round this number to the nearest thousand. Since the hundreds digit is 5, we round up to 5,000. In math, we express this as: Round 4,520 to nearest thousand = 5,000. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. Is 8,500 rounded to the nearest thousand 8,000 or 9,000?

examples.title: Example 24
examples.content: Review standard details: Round 4,520 to nearest thousand = 5,000.

slides.title: Summary of Week 4 Lessons
slides.text: Let's review: we learned to represent, read, and write numbers up to ten thousand, identify place values, round to tens/hundreds/thousands, and compare/order numbers. In math, we express this as: Numbers to 10,000 = read, write, compare, round. Let's learn and master this key mathematical concept. Whole numbers up to ten thousand have four digits: thousands, hundreds, tens, and ones. The position of a digit in a number determines its place value in the base-ten system. We find the value of a digit by multiplying the digit itself by its place value position. When reading a four-digit number, we read the thousands place first, followed by the hundreds. Rounding a number to the nearest ten means checking the ones digit to decide if we round up or down. How do large numbers help us record statistics in South Cotabato?

examples.title: Example 25
examples.content: Review standard details: Numbers to 10,000 = read, write, compare, round.

pages:
Exercise 1: Base-Ten Block Values

Write the number represented by the base-ten block models below.

1. 3 thousands blocks, 5 hundreds sheets, 2 tens rods, and 8 ones blocks =
3528
2. 7 thousands blocks, 0 hundreds sheets, 4 tens rods, and 0 ones blocks =
7040

Exercise 2: Writing Numerals

Write the correct standard numeral for the given number in words.

1. "Five thousand four hundred ninety-two" in numerals:
5492

Exercise 3: Place Value up to 10,000

Identify the place value of the underlined digit.

1. In the number 8,345, what is the place value of the underlined digit 3?
hundreds

performance.title: [Advanced] Tuna Harvest Record Block Drawing
performance.desc: Advanced Task: Represent the number 4,325 (which represents boxes of tuna harvested at the General Santos City port) using drawings of base-ten blocks. Draw 4 large cubes for thousands, 3 flat squares for hundreds, 2 vertical rectangles for tens, and 5 small dots for ones. Color them neatly, write the number in standard form and in words, take a photo, and upload it.
labels: Correct block drawings representing 4,325
Number written in standard form and in words
Clean and neat drawing

slides.title: Scientific Process Integration
slides.text: Scientific research integrates multiple process skills to establish causal relationships and document empirical evidence. For instance, consider the example of Causal Relationship: A link where one event (cause) makes another event happen (effect). For instance, consider the example of Systematic Observation: Observing with standardized protocols to eliminate human bias. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

examples.title: Causal Relationship
examples.content: A link where one event (cause) makes another event happen (effect).

examples.title: Systematic Observation
examples.content: Observing with standardized protocols to eliminate human bias.

slides.title: Advanced Vocabulary
slides.text: Terms of empirical research: Quantitative analysis, Extrapolation, Interpolation, and Correlation. For instance, consider the example of Extrapolation: Estimating values beyond the observed range based on a trend. For instance, consider the example of Correlation: A mutual relationship or connection between two variables. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

examples.content: Estimating values beyond the observed range based on a trend.

examples.title: Correlation
examples.content: A mutual relationship or connection between two variables.

slides.title: Empirical Reasoning
slides.text: Scientists use process skills to construct models, communicate raw data tables, and formulate theories. For instance, consider the example of Scientific Theory: A well-substantiated explanation of some aspect of the natural world. For instance, consider the example of Model Construction: Creating physical or digital representations to study systems. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

slides.title: Process: Botanical Observation
slides.text: Using high-magnification tools to observe plant guard cell behavior under different humidity levels. For instance, consider the example of Stomata: Microscopic pores on leaves that open and close to regulate gas exchange. For instance, consider the example of Osmotic Pressure: The force that drives water in and out of guard cells. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

examples.title: Osmotic Pressure
examples.content: The force that drives water in and out of guard cells.

slides.title: Process: Buoyancy Predictions
slides.text: Predicting displacement volume and buoyant forces using Archimedes' principle. For instance, consider the example of Archimedes Principle: An object floats when the buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid. For instance, consider the example of Volume Displacement: Measuring exactly how much water is pushed aside in cubic centimeters. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

examples.title: Volume Displacement

slides.title: Process: Precise Calibration
slides.text: Measuring liquid meniscus levels with graduated glassware, adjusting for thermal expansion of liquids. For instance, consider the example of Cohesive Forces: Attraction between similar molecules, pulling water into a curve. For instance, consider the example of Thermal Expansion: Liquids expand slightly when heated, changing volume readings. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. As we explore more about this, we will find new ways to use this information to help our community.

slides.text: Classifying organisms using a dichotomous key based on binary morphological characteristics. For instance, consider the example of Dichotomous Key: A tool that allows the user to identify organisms through progressive choices. For instance, consider the example of Morphology: The physical shape and structure of an organism. slides.title: Process: Causal Inferences
slides.text: Differentiating between correlation and causation when observing environmental phenomena. For instance, consider the example of Causation: One event directly producing the other (e.g., gravity causing objects to fall). For instance, consider the example of Coincidence: Two events happening together without a direct causal link. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation. Every example we observe helps us connect theoretical knowledge with practical real-world applications. examples.title: Coincidence

slides.title: Process: Kinetic Observations
slides.text: Observing molecular activity in substances during phase transitions. For instance, consider the example of Kinetic Theory: All matter consists of tiny particles in constant motion. For instance, consider the example of Phase Transitions: Heat changes particle motion, causing solid/liquid/gas shifts. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. slides.title: Common Error: Mistaking Correlation for Causation
slides.text: Assuming that because two trends rise together, one must cause the other. For instance, consider the example of Incorrect: Saying 'Ice cream sales cause sunburns because both rise in summer.' For instance, consider the example of Correct: The sun's heat causes both ice cream sales and sunburns. slides.title: Common Error: Cherry-Picking Data
slides.text: Only selecting data points that confirm your hypothesis while ignoring exceptions. For instance, consider the example of Incorrect: Recording only the fast car runs and ignoring the slow ones. For instance, consider the example of Correct: Calculating the average of all trials, including anomalies. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. standard.q: Which process skill involves estimating values between observed data points on a graph?
What do we call the morphological characteristic key used to identify organisms?
According to Archimedes' principle, what force keeps a boat floating?
options: Buoyant force equal to the displaced water weight

standard.q: Which of the following is a scientific inference based on superposed rock layers?
options: The bottom layer was deposited earlier than the top layer.
The rock layer is 3 meters thick.

standard.q: What does the Law of Conservation of Mass state about chemical reactions?
options: Mass is neither created nor destroyed in a closed system.
Mass turns into energy and disappears.

standard.q: True or False: Correlation always proves that one variable caused the change in another.
Which axis on a standard line graph represents the independent variable?
What is the term for estimating values BEYOND the measured data range?
Why do scientists use a dichotomous key?
options: To systematically identify organisms based on physical characteristics

standard.q: What color does blue litmus paper turn when dipped in an acid?
True or False: Boyle's Law states that gas pressure increases as volume decreases at constant temperature.
Which type of heat transfer occurs through fluid currents?
What is the meniscus point for reading water volume in glass cylinders?
options: The bottom of the concave curve

standard.q: What plant structures open and close to regulate gas exchange?
Why do we stain cells before viewing them under a light microscope?
options: To increase contrast and make cellular structures visible

standard.q: Which of the following is a physical property of a base?
True or False: Newton's Law of Cooling states that cooling rate depends on the temperature difference between object and surroundings.
What organic layer of soil holds nutrients and water for agriculture?
Which tool measures atmospheric pressure trends?
True or False: Extrapolating data carries the risk of assuming trends continue indefinitely without change.
What happens to water density when it freezes into ice?
options: It decreases, which is why ice floats.

standard.q: Which of the following is a morphological characteristic of a leaf?
options: The serrated or smooth edge structure

standard.q: True or False: A closed system prevents mass exchange with the outside environment.
What hormone causes phototropism by gathering on the shaded side of stems?
Which process skill is main tool for identifying rock age in geologic layers?
options: Inferring layer sequence based on superposition laws

challenge.q: A student plots water cooling rates. At 0 mins temp is 90C; at 5 mins it is 70C; at 10 mins it is 55C. What is the interpolated temperature at 7 minutes?
You are using a dichotomous key to identify a tree. The key states: 1a. Needle-like leaves (Pine); 1b. Broad leaves (go to 2). The specimen has broad leaves. What is the next step?
options: Proceed to step 2 in the key.

challenge.q: Why does a closed jar containing vinegar and baking soda weigh the same before and after they react?
options: Mass is conserved because no atoms could escape the sealed container.

challenge.q: What type of error is committed if a weather model predicts sunny weather based on a trend but ignores an incoming low-pressure front?
options: Extrapolation error due to ignoring changing variables

challenge.q: An ecologist wants to classify a biome. The climograph shows low precipitation and high temperatures year-round. What is the classification?

performance.title: Archimedes' Buoyancy & Density Investigation
performance.desc: Select three solid objects (e.g., a plastic toy, a metal bolt, a piece of wood). Measure the mass of each object. Build a water displacement cup to measure the volume of displaced water for each. Calculate the density of each object, write down a prediction about their buoyancy, and explain how the buoyant force relates to water displacement to your parent.
labels: Accurately measured mass and displaced water volume
Correctly calculated density using the mass/volume formula
Wrote a clear, scientific explanation of buoyant force based on Archimedes' principle

pages:
Exercise 1: Dichotomous Key Navigation

Use the dichotomous key below to identify the animal specimen: A vertebrate with feathers and wings.

1a. Has backbone (go to 2) | 1b. No backbone (Invertebrate)
2a. Has feathers (Bird) | 2b. No feathers (go to 3)
What is the classification of the specimen?
Bird

Exercise 2: Interpolation & Extrapolation

Read the graph data table and write the estimated values.

Time (minutes) 0 2 4 6 (estimated)
Plant Height (cm) 10 12 14 16
Was finding the height at 6 minutes an example of interpolation or extrapolation?
Extrapolation

Exercise 3: Conservation of Mass

Verify the law of conservation of mass using experimental values.

Scenario: 10g of baking soda reacts with 50g of vinegar in a closed flask. A gas forms.
1. What is the total mass of the flask contents after the chemical reaction?
60g
2. What scientific law justifies this answer?
Law of Conservation of Mass

english.title: English
english.subtitle: Week 4: Describing Physical Properties & Sentence Review

slides.title: Welcome to the Review Expedition!
slides.text: Hello, Explorer! Welcome to English Week 4! This week, we are going on a review expedition. We will review our three spelling patterns: CVCC, CCVC, and VCV. We will learn about adjectives and how they describe things. We will review declarative, interrogative, and imperative sentences. We will make logical predictions based on clues in nature. Let's keep our notebooks ready to write down answers. Are you ready to show what you have learned? For instance, consider the example of Weekly Goal: Master spelling patterns review, apply adjectives to physical properties, review sentence types, and make predictions. To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: Weekly Goal
examples.content: Master spelling patterns review, apply adjectives to physical properties, review sentence types, and make predictions.

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: Word Pattern Review
slides.text: We have learned three important word patterns this term. CVCC words end in two consonant letters (like hand). CCVC words start with two consonant letters (like frog). VCV words have a consonant between two vowels (like baby). Being able to spell them helps us read quickly. It also helps us write science observations accurately. Let's practice spelling these patterns on our paper. They are the building blocks of our language safari! For instance, consider the example of Review Patterns: CVCC (consonant blends), CCVC (initial blends), VCV (long vowels). To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.

examples.title: Review Patterns
examples.content: CVCC (consonant blends), CCVC (initial blends), VCV (long vowels).

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: CVCC Practice: The Wet Pond
slides.text: Let's walk near the wet pond in the park. We see a green lamp lighting up the path. Can you spot the CVCC words in our sentences? Yes, the words are 'pond' and 'lamp'! Both words end with a blend of two consonants. We spell them correctly as p-o-n-d and l-a-m-p. Listen to the ending sound of both words carefully. They show how CVCC blends are used in nature! For instance, consider the example of Spelling: p - o - n - d and l - a - m - p To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: CCVC Practice: The Crawling Crab
slides.text: We spot a small crab crawling on the beach. It does not stop when it sees us watching. Can you spot the CCVC words in our sentences? Yes, the words are 'crab' and 'stop'! Both words start with a blend of two consonants. We spell them correctly as c-r-a-b and s-t-o-p. Listen to the beginning sound of both words carefully. They show how CCVC blends are used in nature! For instance, consider the example of Spelling: c - r - a - b and s - t - o - p To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems.

examples.title: HOTS Challenge
examples.content: How would you apply this linguistic pattern to create a complex sentence?

slides.title: VCV Practice: The Busy Spider
slides.text: We watch a busy spider spin a web on a tulip. It sits on the paper leaf in the warm sun. Can you spot the VCV words in our sentences? Yes, the words are 'spider', 'tulip', and 'paper'! They have a single consonant between two vowels. We spell them correctly as s-p-i-d-e-r and t-u-l-i-p. Listen to the long vowel sounds in these words. They show how VCV patterns are used in nature! To understand this concept deeply, we must look at how it applies to different situations in our daily lives. By studying this topic, students can develop critical thinking skills and learn to solve complex problems. It is important to practice regularly and discuss these ideas with your peers and teachers to build a strong foundation.
